<gallery> File:Ryukakuji Iwaya Kofun, funkyu-1.jpg|Southwest corner of the mound (stone chamber opening visible in the background) File:Ryukakuji Iwaya Kofun, funkyu-2.jpg|Northwest corner and embankment File:Ryukakuji Iwaya Kofun, entrance-2.jpg|Entrance to the eastern and western chambers File:Ryukakuji Iwaya Kofun, entrance-3.jpg|Entrance to the eastern chamber File:Ryukakuji Iwaya Kofun, entrance-4.jpg|Entrance to the western chamber File:Ryukakuji Iwaya Kofun, genshitsu.jpg|Interior of the western chamber </gallery>
== Ryukakuji Kofun Cluster == Ryukakuji Iwaya Kofun is part of the larger Ryukakuji Kofun Cluster, which contains 114 known Kofuns. Located on the {{ill|Shimōsa Plateau|en|Shimōsa Plateau|WD=Q5120650|lt=Shimōsa Plateau|1=Shimōsa Plateau}} in northeastern Chiba, construction in the cluster is believed to have started in the 6th century with smaller {{ill|Zenpokoenfun|en|Zenpokoenfun|WD=Q11268718|lt=Zenpokoenfun|1=Zenpōkōenfun}} and round Kofuns (円墳). By the early 7th century, larger keyhole-shaped mounds such as the Sengenyama Kofun were built, followed by square mounds like the Ryukakuji Iwaya Kofun.
== Design and Burial Facilities == The mound measures 78 meters per side and rises 13.2 meters high. It features three stepped tiers with a moat surrounding three sides and an embankment beyond. In 2008, surveyors identified a ramp on the southern side leading to the mound.<ref>Jinno (2009), p. 15.</ref>
Both horizontal stone chambers lack passageways, consisting only of burial chambers. The chambers were built using sandstone from the Kinoshita Formation, while schist from {{ill|Mount Tsukuba|en|Mount Tsukuba|WD=Q1754619|lt=Mount Tsukuba|1=Mount Tsukuba}} was used for platforms supporting coffins.<ref>Kurita (2005), p. 159; Shiraishi (2007), pp. 166–167.</ref>
== Timeline == * 1970: Survey by Meiji University to map the mound and chambers. * 2007: Cleaning and survey of Mound 104 by the Sakae Board of Education.
== Historic Site Designation == The site was designated a National Historic Site on January 27, 1941. In 2009, the designation was expanded to include surrounding areas and renamed "Ryukakuji Kofun Cluster and Iwaya Kofun."<ref>[http://www.chibanippo.co.jp/news/chiba/politics_economy_kiji.php?i=nesp1227317452 Chiba Nippo - Ryukakuji Kofun Cluster designated a National Historic Site].</ref>
== Address == 1601 Ikeshita, Ryukakuji, Sakae, Chiba Prefecture, Japan == See Also == {{Commonscat|Ryukakuji Iwaya Kofun}} * [[List of Kofun in Japan]] * [[List of Historic Sites in Kanto]]
== External Links == * [https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/401/643 Ryukakuji Kofun Cluster and Iwaya Kofun - National Cultural Properties Database] * [http://www.town.sakae.chiba.jp/index.php?code=874 Ryukakuji Kofun Cluster List - Sakae Town]

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[co:commons:Category:Ryukakuji Iwaya Kofun]][[ja:龍角寺岩屋古墳]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[co:commons:Category:Ryukakuji Iwaya Kofun]][[ja:龍角寺岩屋古墳]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[Category:sn_crud|TESTcommons:Category:Ryukakuji Iwaya Kofun]][[ja:龍角寺岩屋古墳]]
{{Infobox ancient site of Japan |name=Ryukakuji Iwaya Kofun (龍角寺岩屋古墳) |image=File:Ryukakuji Iwaya Kofun.jpg |location={{ill|Sakae|WD=Q1202754|1=Sakae, Chiba}} |coordinates={{Coord|35.821111|N|140.277222|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} |shape=Square Kofun |dimensions=78 m on each side, 13.2 m in height |construction_period=Early–mid 7th century |burial goods=Haniwa in the shape of waterfowl |designation=National Historic Site (1941) |Burial Facility=Two horizontal stone chambers |map={{Location map|Japan Chiba|lat_deg=35|lat_min=49|lat_sec=16|lon_deg=140|lon_min=16|lon_sec=38|label=Ryukakuji Iwaya Kofun|mark=方墳.svg|marksize=28|caption=Location in Chiba Prefecture|position=bottom|width=200|float=center}} |architectural_styles={{ill|square kofun|WD=Q11504353|1=Square Kofun}} }}
'''Ryukakuji Iwaya Kofun''' (龍角寺岩屋古墳) is a square Kofun (方墳) located in {{ill|Sakae|ja|栄町 (千葉県)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sakae|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, Chiba Prefecture. It is designated as No. 105 in the {{ill|Ryūgakuji Kofun Cluster|en|Ryūgakuji Kofun Cluster|WD=Q11679371|lt=Ryūgakuji Kofun Cluster|1=Ryūgakuji Kofun Cluster}}, which comprises 115 Kofuns. Together with the Ryukakuji Kofun Cluster, it is recognized as a [[Historic Site of Japan]].
== Overview ==
[[File:100501 R Iwaya kofun aerial.jpg|thumb|240px|right|Aerial view of Ryukakuji Iwaya Kofun (2010) from the Geospatial Information Authority of Japan aerial photograph]] The kofun is located on a terrace about 30 meters above sea level, on the northern edge of {{ill|Inbanuma|ja|印旛沼|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Inbanuma|qq=}}, a marsh in Chiba Prefecture. Scholars estimate its construction to the early–mid 7th century, during the late Kofun period.<ref>Kurita (2005), p. 158; Shiraishi (2007), p. 178; Ozawa (2008), p. 336.</ref> However, the lack of unearthed artifacts from the site has led to debates about its precise construction period, with estimations based primarily on the architectural features of the stone chambers.<ref>Ōtsuka (2009), p. 39.</ref> The neighboring {{ill|Sengenyama Kofun (Sakae)|ja|浅間山古墳 (栄町)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sengenyama Kofun (Sakae)|qq=}} is thought to have been built slightly earlier, around the early 7th century.
The mound has three stepped tiers, with each side measuring 78 meters and a height of 13.2 meters. Surrounding it is a 3-meter-wide moat and embankment. With its large size, the kofun surpasses contemporary square mounds such as the {{ill|Kasuga Mukaiyama Kofun|ja|春日向山古墳|1=Kasuga Mukaiyama Kofun|2=ja|3=春日向山古墳|4=de|5=Kasuga Mukaiyama Kofun|6=fr|7=Kasuga Mukaiyama Kofun|1=Kasugayama Kofun|1=Kasuga Mukaiyama Kofun|2=ja|3=春日向山古墳|4=de|5=Kasuga Mukaiyama Kofun|6=fr|7=Kasuga Mukaiyama Kofun|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kasuga Mukaiyama Kofun|13=User:Immanuelle/Kasuga Mukaiyama Kofun|qq=}} (tomb of Emperor Yōmei) and {{ill|Yamada Takatsuka Kofun|ja|山田高塚古墳|1=Yamada Takatsuka Kofun|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamada Takatsuka Kofun|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamada Takatsuka Kofun|qq=}} (tomb of Empress Suiko). Nationally, it Ranks as the second-largest square Kofun after {{ill|Masuyama Kofun|ja|桝山古墳|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Masuyama Kofun|qq=}}, built in the early 5th century in {{ill|Kashihara, Nara|en|Kashihara, Nara|WD=Q496319|lt=Kashihara, Nara|1=Kashihara, Nara}}.<ref>Shiraishi (2007), p. 159.</ref>
The southern face of the mound features two horizontal stone chambers positioned 10 meters apart. The western chamber measures 4.23 meters in length, with a rear wall width of 1.68 meters and a height of 2.14 meters. The eastern chamber, slightly larger, has partially collapsed. Both chambers were constructed with sandstone from the {{ill|Kinoshita Formation|ja|下総層群木下層|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kinoshita Formation|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, known for its abundant fossilized shells.<ref>[https://www.pref.chiba.lg.jp/kyouiku/bunkazai/bunkazai/n431-019.html Kinoshita Formation] (Chiba Prefecture).</ref>
<gallery> File:Ryukakuji Iwaya Kofun, funkyu-1.jpg|Southwest corner of the mound (stone chamber opening visible in the background) File:Ryukakuji Iwaya Kofun, funkyu-2.jpg|Northwest corner and embankment File:Ryukakuji Iwaya Kofun, entrance-2.jpg|Entrance to the eastern and western chambers File:Ryukakuji Iwaya Kofun, entrance-3.jpg|Entrance to the eastern chamber File:Ryukakuji Iwaya Kofun, entrance-4.jpg|Entrance to the western chamber File:Ryukakuji Iwaya Kofun, genshitsu.jpg|Interior of the western chamber </gallery>
== Ryukakuji Kofun Cluster == Ryukakuji Iwaya Kofun is part of the larger Ryukakuji Kofun Cluster, which contains 114 known Kofuns. Located on the {{ill|Shimōsa Plateau|en|Shimōsa Plateau|WD=Q5120650|lt=Shimōsa Plateau|1=Shimōsa Plateau}} in northeastern Chiba, construction in the cluster is believed to have started in the 6th century with smaller {{ill|Zenpokoenfun|en|Zenpokoenfun|WD=Q11268718|lt=Zenpokoenfun|1=Zenpōkōenfun}} and round Kofuns (円墳). By the early 7th century, larger keyhole-shaped mounds such as the Sengenyama Kofun were built, followed by square mounds like the Ryukakuji Iwaya Kofun.
== Design and Burial Facilities == The mound measures 78 meters per side and rises 13.2 meters high. It features three stepped tiers with a moat surrounding three sides and an embankment beyond. In 2008, surveyors identified a ramp on the southern side leading to the mound.<ref>Jinno (2009), p. 15.</ref>
Both horizontal stone chambers lack passageways, consisting only of burial chambers. The chambers were built using sandstone from the Kinoshita Formation, while schist from {{ill|Mount Tsukuba|en|Mount Tsukuba|WD=Q1754619|lt=Mount Tsukuba|1=Mount Tsukuba}} was used for platforms supporting coffins.<ref>Kurita (2005), p. 159; Shiraishi (2007), pp. 166–167.</ref>
== Timeline == * 1970: Survey by Meiji University to map the mound and chambers. * 2007: Cleaning and survey of Mound 104 by the Sakae Board of Education.
== Historic Site Designation == The site was designated a National Historic Site on January 27, 1941. In 2009, the designation was expanded to include surrounding areas and renamed "Ryukakuji Kofun Cluster and Iwaya Kofun."<ref>[http://www.chibanippo.co.jp/news/chiba/politics_economy_kiji.php?i=nesp1227317452 Chiba Nippo - Ryukakuji Kofun Cluster designated a National Historic Site].</ref>

Scholars estimate its construction to the early–mid 7th century, during the late Kofun period.<ref>Kurita (2005), p. 158; Shiraishi (2007), p. 178; Ozawa (2008), p. 336.</ref> However, the lack of unearthed artifacts from the site has led to debates about its precise construction period, with estimations based primarily on the architectural features of the stone chambers.<ref>Ōtsuka (2009), p. 39.</ref> The neighboring {{ill|Sengenyama Kofun (Sakae)|ja|浅間山古墳 (栄町)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sengenyama Kofun (Sakae)|qq=}} is thought to have been built slightly earlier, around the early 7th century.
The mound has three stepped tiers, with each side measuring 78 meters and a height of 13.2 meters. Surrounding it is a 3-meter-wide moat and embankment. With its large size, the kofun surpasses contemporary square mounds such as the {{ill|Kasuga Mukaiyama Kofun|ja|春日向山古墳|1=Kasuga Mukaiyama Kofun|2=ja|3=春日向山古墳|4=de|5=Kasuga Mukaiyama Kofun|6=fr|7=Kasuga Mukaiyama Kofun|1=Kasugayama Kofun|1=Kasuga Mukaiyama Kofun|2=ja|3=春日向山古墳|4=de|5=Kasuga Mukaiyama Kofun|6=fr|7=Kasuga Mukaiyama Kofun|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kasuga Mukaiyama Kofun|13=User:Immanuelle/Kasuga Mukaiyama Kofun|qq=}} (tomb of Emperor Yōmei) and {{ill|Yamada Takatsuka Kofun|ja|山田高塚古墳|1=Yamada Takatsuka Kofun|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamada Takatsuka Kofun|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamada Takatsuka Kofun|qq=}} (tomb of Empress Suiko). Nationally, it Ranks as the second-largest square Kofun after {{ill|Masuyama Kofun|ja|桝山古墳|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Masuyama Kofun|qq=}}, built in the early 5th century in {{ill|Kashihara, Nara|en|Kashihara, Nara|WD=Q496319|lt=Kashihara, Nara|1=Kashihara, Nara}}.<ref>Shiraishi (2007), p. 159.</ref>
The southern face of the mound features two horizontal stone chambers positioned 10 meters apart. The western chamber measures 4.23 meters in length, with a rear wall width of 1.68 meters and a height of 2.14 meters. The eastern chamber, slightly larger, has partially collapsed. Both chambers were constructed with sandstone from the {{ill|Kinoshita Formation|ja|下総層群木下層|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kinoshita Formation|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, known for its abundant fossilized shells.<ref>[https://www.pref.chiba.lg.jp/kyouiku/bunkazai/bunkazai/n431-019.html Kinoshita Formation] (Chiba Prefecture).</ref>
<gallery> File:Ryukakuji Iwaya Kofun, funkyu-1.jpg|Southwest corner of the mound (stone chamber opening visible in the background) File:Ryukakuji Iwaya Kofun, funkyu-2.jpg|Northwest corner and embankment File:Ryukakuji Iwaya Kofun, entrance-2.jpg|Entrance to the eastern and western chambers File:Ryukakuji Iwaya Kofun, entrance-3.jpg|Entrance to the eastern chamber File:Ryukakuji Iwaya Kofun, entrance-4.jpg|Entrance to the western chamber File:Ryukakuji Iwaya Kofun, genshitsu.jpg|Interior of the western chamber </gallery>
== Ryukakuji Kofun Cluster == Ryukakuji Iwaya Kofun is part of the larger Ryukakuji Kofun Cluster, which contains 114 known Kofuns. Located on the {{ill|Shimōsa Plateau|en|Shimōsa Plateau|WD=Q5120650|lt=Shimōsa Plateau|1=Shimōsa Plateau}} in northeastern Chiba, construction in the cluster is believed to have started in the 6th century with smaller {{ill|Zenpokoenfun|en|Zenpokoenfun|WD=Q11268718|lt=Zenpokoenfun|1=Zenpōkōenfun}} and round Kofuns (円墳). By the early 7th century, larger keyhole-shaped mounds such as the Sengenyama Kofun were built, followed by square mounds like the Ryukakuji Iwaya Kofun.
== Design and Burial Facilities == The mound measures 78 meters per side and rises 13.2 meters high. It features three stepped tiers with a moat surrounding three sides and an embankment beyond. In 2008, surveyors identified a ramp on the southern side leading to the mound.<ref>Jinno (2009), p. 15.</ref>
Both horizontal stone chambers lack passageways, consisting only of burial chambers. The chambers were built using sandstone from the Kinoshita Formation, while schist from {{ill|Mount Tsukuba|en|Mount Tsukuba|WD=Q1754619|lt=Mount Tsukuba|1=Mount Tsukuba}} was used for platforms supporting coffins.<ref>Kurita (2005), p. 159; Shiraishi (2007), pp. 166–167.</ref>
== Timeline == * 1970: Survey by Meiji University to map the mound and chambers. * 2007: Cleaning and survey of Mound 104 by the Sakae Board of Education.
== Historic Site Designation == The site was designated a National Historic Site on January 27, 1941. In 2009, the designation was expanded to include surrounding areas and renamed "Ryukakuji Kofun Cluster and Iwaya Kofun."<ref>[http://www.chibanippo.co.jp/news/chiba/politics_economy_kiji.php?i=nesp1227317452 Chiba Nippo - Ryukakuji Kofun Cluster designated a National Historic Site].</ref>
